content.full_text:
FAIR p OLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Phillip A. Tate 
Sheppard Mullin 
Richter & Hampton LLP 
333 South Hope Street, 43rd Fl. 
Los Angeles, CA 90071-1422 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our file No. W-11-206 
Dear Mr. Tate: 
January 9,2011 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 I attempted to contact you by telephone last 
month, and I left you a voicemail message when it appeared that you were unavailable at the 
time of my call. As I understand the facts given in your request for advice, you wrote on behalf 
of a commercial enterprise, a client you characterized as a "real party in interest" to a 
governmental decision because of its potential economic consequences for your client. 
The Act (Section 831 l 4(b) and Regulation 18329, copies enclosed) authorizes a response 
to a request for formal written advice or informal written assistance only when the person 
seeking help is a person whose duties under the Act are in question, or is the authorized 
representative of such a person. 
It does not appear that your client is a person whose duties under the Act are in question. 
Thus, under the provisions of Regulation 18329(b)(8)(B) and (c)(4)(C), we are unable to respond 
to your request in this matter. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18 l l O through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


F il e  No. W-11-20 6

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a commercial enterprise that would be affected by a governmental decision can request FPPC advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to respond to the request because the requestor's client is not a person whose duties under the Act are in question, and the FPPC can only provide advice to such persons or their authorized representatives under Regulation 18329.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a declination letter rather than a full advice letter. The document does not contain explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections with verbatim text, but the advisory nature and reasoning are clear from the body of the letter. The letter explains the regulatory basis for the declination (Regulation 18329) and invites resubmission if the requestor is representing someone whose duties under the Act are in question.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a commercial enterprise that would be affected by a governmental decision can request FPPC advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC declined to respond to the request because the requestor's client is not a person whose duties under the Act are in question, and the FPPC can only provide advice to such persons or their authorized representatives under Regulation 18329.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declined to provide written advice to an attorney seeking guidance on behalf of a commercial enterprise regarding conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act, as the client does not have duties under the Act and therefore does not qualify for FPPC assistance under Regulation 18329.
